does: This paper investigates inverse scaling phenomena in large language models when given additional test-time compute resources. The authors examine scenarios where increased computational budget during inference—through techniques like chain-of-thought, search, or sampling—paradoxically leads to worse model performance rather than improvements. They systematically study this inverse scaling behavior across different model scales, tasks, and computational budgets, identifying which problem types and model configurations are most susceptible to performance degradation. The work provides empirical characterization of when and why test-time compute fails to help, contrary to the typical assumption that more computation improves reasoning.
connects: This work extends research on test-time scaling and emergent capabilities in language models, building on prior investigations of scaling laws and compute allocation. It relates to studies of chain-of-thought prompting and iterative refinement methods that aim to improve performance through additional inference-time computation. The paper connects to broader literature on inverse scaling phenomena in neural networks, where specific task formulations or model behaviors lead to worse performance with scale. It also engages with research on preference collapse, mode connectivity, and how models can get stuck in unproductive reasoning paths, as well as work examining failure modes of reasoning approaches in LLMs.
enables: This research opens directions for understanding failure modes of test-time scaling and designing more robust inference procedures. It enables better prediction of which tasks and scenarios benefit from test-time compute investment versus those where it becomes counterproductive. The findings motivate development of improved sampling and search strategies that avoid inverse scaling traps, potentially through better reward models, verification mechanisms, or curriculum-based approaches. It points toward meta-learning or adaptive allocation methods that could dynamically adjust test-time computation. The work also establishes a foundation for understanding the relationship between model scale, task properties, and inference strategy effectiveness, informing future architectural and training decisions.
